title: Cybersecurity risk-management measures
content: Member States shall ensure that essential and important entities take appropriate and proportionate technical, operational and organisational measures to manage the risks posed to the security of network and information systems which those entities use for their operations or for the provision of their services, and to prevent or minimise the impact of incidents on recipients of their services and on other services. The measures shall ensure a level of security of network and information systems appropriate to the risks posed, taking into account the state of the art, applicable European and international standards, and the costs of implementation. Member States shall ensure that competent authorities take into account the guidelines issued by ENISA when assessing whether the measures taken by essential and important entities are appropriate.

title: Governance - management body accountability
content: Member States shall ensure that the management bodies of essential and important entities approve the cybersecurity risk-management measures taken by those entities in accordance with Article 21 and oversee their implementation. Member States shall ensure that members of the management bodies of essential and important entities are required to follow training, and shall encourage essential and important entities to offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order to gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices and their impact on the services provided by the entity. Management bodies can be held personally liable for infringements of NIS2 obligations.

title: Security measures - technical and organisational requirements
content: Member States shall ensure that essential and important entities take appropriate and proportionate technical and organisational measures to manage cybersecurity risks. Those measures shall be based on an all-hazards approach and shall include at least: (a) policies on risk analysis and information system security; (b) incident handling; (c) business continuity, backup management and disaster recovery, and crisis management; (d) supply chain security including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers; (e) security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance, including vulnerability handling and disclosure; (f) policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures; (g) basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training; (h) policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption; (i) human resources security, access control policies and asset management; (j) the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, secured voice, video and text communications, and secured emergency communication systems.

title: Incident reporting obligations and timelines
content: Member States shall ensure that essential and important entities notify the competent authority or CSIRT without undue delay of any incident having a significant impact on the provision of their services. Notification timelines: (1) Early warning within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident - indicating whether the incident is suspected to be caused by unlawful or malicious action. (2) Incident notification within 72 hours of becoming aware - updating the early warning and providing an initial assessment including severity and impact. (3) Intermediate report on request from the competent authority or CSIRT. (4) Final report no later than one month after the incident notification. A significant incident is one that has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of services or financial loss for the entity, or has affected or is capable of affecting other natural or legal persons by causing considerable material or non-material damage.

title: Supervisory measures and sanctions - fines
content: For essential entities, NIS2 provides for administrative fines of at least €10,000,000 or at least 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the undertaking to which the essential entity belongs in the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. For important entities, administrative fines of at least €7,000,000 or at least 1.4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. Competent authorities may also impose temporary bans on management bodies from exercising managerial functions in essential entities for repeated infringements. Member States shall ensure that competent authorities can apply these supervisory measures: on-site inspections, regular and targeted security audits, requests for information, and access to data, documents and other information necessary for the performance of their tasks.

title: Supply chain security obligations
content: Member States shall ensure that, when assessing the security of supply chains for ICT products, ICT services and ICT processes, entities take into account: (a) vulnerability-specific risk-assessment results of individual suppliers and service providers; (b) the overall quality of products and cybersecurity practices of the supplier or service provider; (c) the ability of the supplier or service provider to comply with applicable cybersecurity obligations by contract. Essential and important entities must implement security measures covering their relationships with direct suppliers and service providers, including clauses on incident notification and information sharing. The supply chain security requirements under NIS2 create downstream obligations on vendors and managed service providers supplying critical-sector entities.

title: Integrity and confidentiality principle
content: Personal data shall be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality' principle). This principle sits alongside the other six UK GDPR data protection principles and applies to all controllers and processors. Failure to implement appropriate security measures that results in a breach directly engages this principle and is likely to constitute a violation independent of any breach notification obligation.

title: Security of processing - technical and organisational measures
content: Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. Controllers and processors must assess risks presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed.

title: Breach notification to ICO - 72 hour obligation
content: In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority (the ICO in the UK), unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Where the notification to the supervisory authority is not made within 72 hours, it shall be accompanied by reasons for the delay. The notification shall at least: (a) describe the nature of the breach including categories and approximate number of data subjects and records concerned; (b) communicate the name and contact details of the data protection officer; (c) describe the likely consequences of the breach; (d) describe the measures taken or proposed to address the breach. The processor must notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a breach.

title: Communication of personal data breach to data subjects
content: When the personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay. The communication shall describe in clear and plain language the nature of the personal data breach and contain at least the name and contact details of the DPO or other contact point; description of the likely consequences; description of the measures taken or proposed. Communication to data subjects is not required if: (a) the controller has implemented appropriate technical and organisational protection measures (e.g. encryption); (b) the controller has taken subsequent measures which ensure that the high risk is no longer likely to materialise; (c) it would involve disproportionate effort, in which case a public communication or similar measure may be used instead.

title: Administrative fines - up to £17.5M or 4% global turnover
content: The ICO may impose administrative fines in two tiers: Tier 1 (higher tier): up to £17,500,000 or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover of the preceding financial year, whichever is higher - for infringements of the basic principles for processing (Articles 5-7), conditions for consent, data subjects' rights (Articles 12-22), transfers to third countries (Articles 44-49), and obligations under Member State law. Tier 2 (lower tier): up to £8,700,000 or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher - for obligations of the controller and processor including security of processing (Article 32), breach notification (Articles 33-34), data protection by design, DPO obligations, and obligations of certification bodies. The ICO considers the nature, gravity, duration, intentional or negligent character, categories of personal data affected, and any action taken to mitigate damage.

title: ICT risk management framework - governance and organisation
content: Financial entities shall have in place an internal governance and control framework that ensures an effective and prudent management of ICT risk. The management body of the financial entity shall define, approve, oversee and be responsible for the implementation of all arrangements related to the ICT risk management framework. The management body shall bear the overall responsibility for managing the financial entity's ICT risk and shall define the appropriate level of risk tolerance for ICT risk. Financial entities shall allocate and regularly review the appropriate budget to fulfil their digital operational resilience needs in respect of all types of resources, including relevant ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training. Applies to: credit institutions, payment institutions, e-money institutions, investment firms, crypto-asset service providers, central securities depositories, insurance and reinsurance undertakings, and ICT third-party service providers.

title: ICT risk management framework - components
content: Financial entities shall have a sound, comprehensive and well-documented ICT risk management framework as part of their overall risk management system. The ICT risk management framework shall include strategies, policies, procedures, IT protocols and tools necessary to protect all information and ICT assets, including computer software, hardware and servers, and premises, against ICT risks, as well as strategies, policies, procedures, IT protocols and tools to protect the financial entity against ICT-related incidents. The framework shall enable the financial entity to: identify, classify and adequately document all ICT supported business functions, roles and responsibilities; identify all sources of ICT risk; implement a comprehensive information security policy; detect anomalous activities; have documented response and recovery plans.

title: Protection - security of network and information systems
content: Financial entities shall continuously monitor and control the security and functioning of ICT systems and tools. Financial entities shall minimise the impact of ICT risk by deploying appropriate tools, policies and procedures. Financial entities shall design, procure and implement ICT security policies, procedures, protocols and tools that aim to ensure the resilience, continuity and availability of ICT systems, in particular for those supporting critical or important functions, and to maintain high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data whether at rest, in use or in transit. Specific requirements include: strong authentication mechanisms, dedicated and secure management of ICT systems, data access policies, network segmentation, data leakage prevention policies, and software patching and update policies.

title: Detection of anomalous activities and ICT-related incidents
content: Financial entities shall have in place mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities, including ICT network performance issues and ICT-related incidents, and to identify potential material single points of failure. All detection mechanisms referred to above shall be regularly tested in accordance with Article 25. Financial entities shall devote sufficient resources and capabilities to monitor user activity, the occurrence of ICT anomalies and ICT-related incidents, in particular cyber-attacks. Data providers of critical benchmarks shall, on a best-efforts basis, continuously monitor relevant third-party information sources for the purpose of detecting, analysing and preparing for the impact of cyber threats on their services.

title: Response and recovery - business continuity
content: Financial entities shall put in place a comprehensive ICT business continuity policy as part of the ICT risk management framework. ICT business continuity policy shall include: (a) conditions for activating business continuity arrangements; (b) procedures for response and recovery; (c) communication plans; (d) testing requirements. The ICT business continuity policy shall be reviewed at least annually and following significant ICT-related incidents. Financial entities shall designate a crisis management function which shall, in the case of activation of the ICT business continuity plans, set out clear procedures to manage internal and external crisis communications. Recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) shall be defined for each critical function.

title: Backup policies and recovery procedures
content: For the purpose of ensuring the restoration of ICT systems and data with minimum downtime, limited disruption and loss, as part of their ICT risk management framework, financial entities shall develop and document backup policies and procedures, and restoration and recovery procedures and methods. Financial entities shall: activate backup plans that enable the restoration of data and systems; use ICT systems that are sufficiently redundant to provide adequate capacity and to ensure continuity. Recovery capabilities shall be tested regularly. Backup data shall be stored at a separate location from the primary system, shall not be continuously connected to it, and shall be protected to the same standard as the primary data. Financial entities shall establish the maximum tolerable period of business disruption and the acceptable data loss levels for each critical or important function.

title: ICT-related incident classification and reporting
content: Financial entities shall classify ICT-related incidents and shall determine their impact based on the following criteria: the number of clients or financial counterparts affected; the duration of the ICT-related incident; the geographical spread; the data losses; the criticality of the services affected; the economic impact. Major incidents shall be reported to the competent authority without undue delay. Initial notification: as soon as possible and no later than 4 hours from the moment the financial entity classified the incident as major, and no later than 24 hours from when it became aware of the incident. Intermediate report: within 72 hours of the initial notification. Final report: within one month of the intermediate report. Fines for non-compliance with DORA can reach 1% of average daily worldwide turnover of the preceding business year, applied daily until compliance for ICT third-party providers.

title: Third-party ICT risk - key principles
content: Financial entities shall manage third-party ICT risk as an integral component of their ICT risk framework and in accordance with the proportionality principle. Financial entities shall maintain and update a register of information in relation to all contractual arrangements with ICT third-party service providers. Before entering into contractual arrangements with ICT third-party service providers, financial entities shall: identify and assess all relevant risks; exercise due diligence; identify and assess all conflicts of interest. For critical ICT third-party service providers, the ESAs (EBA, EIOPA, ESMA) designate a Lead Overseer and can impose periodic penalty payments of up to 1% of average daily worldwide turnover.

title: Sanctions and administrative penalties
content: Competent authorities shall have the power to impose administrative penalties and remedial measures for infringements of DORA. For financial entities: penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. For ICT third-party service providers designated as critical: the Lead Overseer may impose periodic penalty payments of up to 1% of the average daily worldwide turnover in the preceding business year, for each day of non-compliance during a period of no more than six months. Member States may allow for criminal penalties for the most serious infringements. The competent authority shall publish decisions imposing administrative penalties on its official website within five working days of the decision becoming final.

title: Application and purpose of operational risk rules
content: SYSC 13 applies to every firm to which SYSC 4 to 10 apply. It sets out rules and guidance relating to the management of operational risk - the risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems or from external events. A firm must take reasonable care to establish and maintain effective systems and controls for operational risk management, including appropriate arrangements for business continuity. Operational risk includes technology and cyber risk as a significant subset. The FCA expects firms to treat operational resilience as a board-level priority with clear accountability. Firms should have documented strategies for operational risk management that are reviewed and approved by the management body at least annually.

title: Management responsibility for operational risk
content: A firm's senior management is responsible for implementing the firm's risk management strategies and policies as approved by the management body. Senior management must maintain adequate monitoring and reporting systems to enable the management body to carry out its responsibilities. Responsibilities should be clearly documented and understood. The management body should approve strategies, policies and frameworks for the management of operational risk, including cyber and technology risk. The FCA's Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR) places individual accountability on senior managers for operational risk failures within their area of responsibility, with potential enforcement action including prohibition orders and financial penalties.

title: Outsourcing and third-party operational risk
content: A firm must take reasonable care to supervise the discharge of outsourced functions and should ensure that outsourcing arrangements do not impair the quality of the firm's internal controls or the ability of the FCA to monitor the firm's compliance. A firm should monitor the performance of any service provider to which it has outsourced a critical or important function. Material outsourcing arrangements must be notified to the FCA in advance. The firm remains fully responsible for complying with its obligations to the FCA even when using third-party providers. Firms should conduct appropriate due diligence on third-party providers, establish contractual protections including audit rights and information security obligations, and have contingency plans for provider failure.

title: Business continuity requirements
content: A firm must take reasonable care to establish and maintain adequate business continuity policies and procedures with a view to ensuring, as far as reasonably practicable, that in the event of an unforeseeable interruption to its normal activities, it can continue to perform its critical functions and minimise losses to itself and others. Business continuity plans should be regularly tested and reviewed. Firms should identify critical business services and set impact tolerances for their disruption. The FCA Operational Resilience Policy (PS21/3) requires all in-scope firms to have identified their important business services and set impact tolerances by March 2022, and to remain within those tolerances no later than March 2025.

title: Information security and cyber risk
content: A firm should establish, implement and document an appropriate information security policy that includes at minimum: access controls and authentication; data classification and handling; patch management; incident detection and response; staff training and awareness. The FCA expects firms to have appropriate cyber controls commensurate with the nature, scale and complexity of their business, and to be able to demonstrate these to supervisors. Firms are required to notify the FCA as soon as reasonably practicable of any material cyber incident that could affect the firm's ability to provide services, cause significant client harm, or affect the integrity of the financial system. The FCA can impose unlimited financial penalties on authorised firms for operational failures and may require firms to take remedial action.

title: FCA notification obligations for operational incidents
content: Firms must notify the FCA of any significant operational incident as soon as reasonably practicable after they become aware of it. Significance is determined by factors including: potential disruption to multiple clients; impact on firm's financial position; involvement of malicious actors; media coverage; systemic risk implications. The FCA defines material cyber incidents as those that result in significant loss of data, availability or control of IT systems; affect a large number of customers; result in unauthorised access to or abuse of a significant number of customer accounts; or cause significant harm to the firm's reputation. Failure to notify the FCA promptly of a material incident may itself constitute a breach of FCA rules and lead to enforcement action separate from the underlying incident.

title: A1 - Governance
content: The organisation has effective organisational structures, policies and processes in place to understand, assess and systematically manage security risks to the network and information systems supporting essential services. Indicators of good practice: the board takes active ownership of cyber risk; a named individual at board or senior management level is accountable for cybersecurity; there is a documented cybersecurity policy approved by the board; cybersecurity is integrated into enterprise risk management; the organisation can demonstrate how it monitors and manages cyber risk at board level. The CAF is used by UK regulators including Ofcom, Ofwat, Ofgem, the FCA, and the CMA to assess organisations operating essential services under NIS Regulations 2018.

title: A2 - Risk management
content: The organisation takes appropriate steps to identify, assess and understand security risks to its network and information systems that support the delivery of the essential service. Indicators of good practice: a systematic risk assessment approach is in place; risks are identified and assessed in the context of the essential service; risk assessments are regularly reviewed and updated; there is documented evidence of risk treatment decisions; the organisation understands its dependencies on third parties and supply chain risks; vulnerabilities are systematically identified and managed. Risk management failures are a primary driver of regulatory enforcement action under the NIS Regulations, with maximum fines of £17 million for essential service operators.

title: A3 - Asset management
content: Everything required to deliver, maintain or support network and information systems necessary for the essential service is determined and understood, with the security of these assets appropriately managed across their lifecycle. Indicators of good practice: a current and accurate asset inventory is maintained; assets are classified based on their importance to the essential service; security requirements are defined for each asset class; asset lifecycle management includes secure disposal; shadow IT and unmanaged assets are identified and addressed; critical assets have enhanced protection proportionate to their importance.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B1 - Service protection policies and processes
content: The organisation defines, implements, communicates and enforces appropriate policies and processes that direct its overall approach to securing the network and information systems supporting the essential service. Indicators: documented security policies covering all critical aspects of the network and information systems; policies are approved at board level; policies are reviewed at least annually; compliance with policies is actively monitored; exceptions are risk-assessed and formally approved; policies are communicated to all relevant staff.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B2 - Identity and access control
content: The organisation understands, documents and controls access to networks and information systems supporting the essential service. User accounts, especially privileged accounts, are managed and only provide the access required. Indicators: all users are uniquely identified and authenticated; privilege is managed on a least privilege basis; privileged accounts are strictly controlled and monitored; multi-factor authentication is used for all remote access and privileged accounts; access rights are reviewed regularly; stale accounts are removed promptly; service accounts are managed and have minimal required privileges.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B3 - Data security
content: Data stored or transmitted electronically is protected from actions that could cause unacceptable impact on the delivery of the essential service. Indicators: data is classified based on sensitivity and criticality; data at rest and in transit is protected using appropriate cryptography; data handling policies are documented and enforced; backups of critical data are maintained, tested and stored separately; data loss prevention controls are in place; media handling and disposal procedures are defined; data transferred to third parties is appropriately protected.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B4 - System security
content: Network and information systems that support the delivery of the essential service are protected from cyber attack. Indicators: systems are designed with security in mind including secure configuration baselines; software vulnerabilities are managed through a structured patch management process; systems are protected by appropriate network controls including firewalls and network monitoring; security testing is conducted regularly; change management processes include security assessment; secure development practices are followed for any custom code; end-of-life systems are identified and have compensating controls or replacement plans.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B5 - Resilient networks and systems
content: The organisation builds resilience against cyber attack into the design, implementation, operation and management of systems that support the delivery of essential services. Indicators: network architecture limits the impact of a compromise; systems supporting the essential service are designed and operated with resilience requirements in mind; single points of failure are identified and mitigated; redundancy and failover mechanisms are tested; suppliers and dependencies are assessed for resilience; recovery capabilities are regularly tested against realistic scenarios.

article_ref: Objective B - Protecting against cyber attack
title: B6 - Staff awareness and training
content: Staff have appropriate awareness and skills to support the security of network and information systems essential to the delivery of services. Indicators: all staff receive appropriate security awareness training; staff with privileged access or security-specific roles receive role-appropriate training; training is regularly updated to reflect current threats; phishing simulation exercises are conducted; security culture is actively promoted from the top; specialist security skills are maintained in-house or through trusted third parties; the organisation can demonstrate how security awareness is measured and improved over time.

article_ref: Objective C - Detecting cyber security events
title: C1 - Security monitoring
content: The organisation monitors the security status of the networks and systems supporting the delivery of the essential service in order to detect potential security problems and track the ongoing effectiveness of protective security measures. Indicators: a monitoring strategy is in place that covers critical systems and networks; logs are collected from key systems and security tools; log data is reviewed in a timely manner; alerting thresholds are defined and tuned; monitoring covers both technical events and user behaviour; logs are protected from tampering and retained for an appropriate period; a Security Operations Centre (SOC) or equivalent function provides ongoing monitoring capability.

article_ref: Objective C - Detecting cyber security events
title: C2 - Proactive threat discovery
content: The organisation detects, within networks and information systems, malicious activity that has bypassed preventative security measures, or may harm the business. Indicators: threat hunting activities are conducted regularly; threat intelligence is used to inform detection capabilities and searches; indicators of compromise are actively searched for across the environment; vulnerability scanning and penetration testing are conducted regularly; the organisation participates in relevant threat intelligence sharing communities (e.g. CiSP, ISACs); anomaly detection tools are deployed on critical systems.

article_ref: Objective D - Minimising the impact of cyber security incidents
title: D1 - Response and recovery planning
content: The organisation has a well-tested incident management process in place, that helps it respond to and recover from cyber incidents affecting the operation of its essential service. Indicators: a documented incident response plan covers the full lifecycle from detection to recovery; roles and responsibilities in the incident response process are clearly defined; playbooks exist for likely incident types including ransomware and supply chain compromise; the plan is tested at least annually through exercises; lessons learned from incidents and exercises are incorporated into improvements; communication plans cover internal stakeholders, regulators, and (where relevant) customers.

article_ref: Objective D - Minimising the impact of cyber security incidents
title: D2 - Lessons learned
content: When a cyber incident occurs, the organisation takes steps to understand the root causes and uses the findings to improve security arrangements. Indicators: post-incident reviews are conducted for all significant incidents; findings are documented and tracked through to resolution; root cause analysis is performed; improvements are prioritised and resourced; lessons learned are shared with appropriate internal stakeholders and, where appropriate, with sector peers and the NCSC; the organisation can demonstrate a cycle of continuous improvement driven by incident learning; near-misses are also reviewed and used as learning opportunities.
